questions.stem: Which adaptation of capillaries aids the exchange of materials?

options.text: A wide lumen with no branching

questions.explanation: Capillaries have thin walls, a large surface area from branching and narrow diameters, aiding exchange.

questions.scenario: Some capillaries have tiny pores in their walls where rapid exchange is needed.
questions.stem: These pores are called:
questions.explanation: Fenestrations are pores in some capillaries where exchange needs to be particularly rapid.

questions.scenario: In a micrograph, one vessel has a thick wall relative to a small lumen.
questions.stem: This vessel is most likely a(n):
questions.explanation: Arteries have thick walls relative to a small lumen; veins have thinner walls and a larger lumen.

questions.stem: The thick layers of muscle and elastic tissue in artery walls help them to:

options.text: Withstand and maintain high blood pressure

questions.explanation: Muscle and elastic tissue allow arteries to withstand and maintain the high pressures of blood leaving the heart.

questions.scenario: A student measures heart rate by feeling a pulse.
questions.stem: Which is a suitable site to feel the pulse?

options.text: The radial artery at the wrist

questions.explanation: Heart rate can be measured by feeling the radial (wrist) or carotid (neck) pulse with the fingertips.

questions.stem: Which adaptation of veins prevents the backflow of blood?
questions.explanation: Veins have valves to prevent backflow and flexible walls that can be compressed by muscle action.

questions.scenario: Blood in veins is often at low pressure.
questions.stem: How is blood helped back to the heart?

options.text: Compression of veins by surrounding muscles, with valves preventing backflow
High pressure generated in the veins themselves

questions.explanation: Muscle action compresses the flexible veins, and valves ensure blood flows only toward the heart.

questions.scenario: The coronary arteries become narrowed by fatty deposits.
questions.stem: This occlusion can lead to:

options.text: Improved oxygen supply to the heart

questions.explanation: Occlusion of the coronary arteries reduces blood supply to the heart muscle, causing coronary heart disease.

questions.scenario: A study finds a strong correlation between saturated fat intake and coronary heart disease.
questions.stem: What can be concluded?

options.text: A strong correlation does not by itself prove a causal link
Saturated fat definitely causes the disease

questions.explanation: Even strong correlations do not prove causation; correlation coefficients quantify the relationship's strength only.

questions.scenario: Water evaporates from leaf cell walls during transpiration.
questions.stem: What is the direct effect on the xylem?

options.text: Water is drawn up under tension (negative pressure)
Water is pushed up under positive pressure only

questions.explanation: Transpiration generates tension (negative pressure) that draws a continuous column of water up the xylem.

questions.stem: Which property of water keeps the column in the xylem unbroken under tension?
questions.explanation: Cohesion between water molecules maintains a continuous column that can be drawn up under tension.

questions.stem: Which adaptation of xylem vessels allows unimpeded water flow?

options.text: Lack of cell contents and incomplete/absent end walls

questions.explanation: Xylem vessels lack cell contents and have incomplete or absent end walls, allowing unimpeded water flow.

questions.stem: Lignified walls in xylem vessels are important because they:

options.text: Withstand the tensions during water transport

questions.explanation: Lignified walls give strength so xylem can withstand the tensions generated during transpiration.

questions.scenario: A student draws a plan diagram of a dicot stem.
questions.stem: Which tissues would be identified?

options.text: Vascular bundles (xylem and phloem), cortex and epidermis

questions.explanation: A dicot stem plan diagram identifies vascular bundles (xylem and phloem), cortex and epidermis.

questions.stem: In a transverse section of a dicot root, the vascular tissue is typically found:

options.text: In the centre of the root

questions.explanation: In dicot roots, the xylem and phloem are located centrally, surrounded by the cortex and epidermis.

questions.scenario: In a micrograph a vessel has a thin wall and a relatively large lumen.
questions.stem: This is most likely a:
questions.explanation: Veins have thin walls relative to a large lumen, unlike the thick-walled, narrow-lumen arteries.

questions.stem: Why do arteries need elastic tissue in their walls?

options.text: To stretch and recoil, smoothing pressure surges from the heart

questions.explanation: Elastic tissue lets arteries stretch and recoil, helping withstand and maintain high blood pressures.

questions.stem: Why are capillaries so numerous and branched?

options.text: To provide a large surface area for exchange
To carry blood at high pressure

questions.explanation: Extensive branching gives capillaries a large surface area and narrow diameters for efficient exchange.

questions.stem: The transport of water from roots to leaves is driven mainly by:
questions.explanation: Transpiration from the leaves generates tension that draws water up the xylem; cohesion keeps the column intact.

questions.scenario: A student compares feeling the pulse manually with using a digital heart-rate monitor.
questions.stem: This comparison is an example of:

options.text: Comparing traditional and digital measurement methods

questions.explanation: Determining heart rate can compare traditional (fingertip pulse) methods with digital ones.

questions.stem: Pits in xylem vessel walls allow:

options.text: Water to enter and exit the vessel

questions.explanation: Pits are gaps in the lignified wall that allow water to enter and exit xylem vessels.

questions.stem: In a dicot stem, xylem and phloem are grouped together in structures called:
questions.explanation: In dicot stems the xylem and phloem are arranged in vascular bundles.

questions.stem: Which statement about veins is correct?

options.text: They return blood to the heart and contain valves
They carry blood away from the heart at high pressure
They have the thickest walls of all vessels
They exchange gases directly with tissues

questions.explanation: Veins return blood to the heart and have valves to prevent backflow.

questions.stem: What is a correlation coefficient used for?

options.text: To quantify the strength of a relationship between variables

questions.explanation: Correlation coefficients quantify how strongly variables are related, but do not by themselves prove causation.

questions.stem: The narrow diameter of capillaries helps exchange by:

options.text: Slowing blood flow and bringing cells close to the wall
Speeding blood past too fast to exchange

questions.explanation: Narrow capillaries slow flow and keep blood cells near the thin walls, aiding exchange.

questions.stem: Compared with veins, arteries have:

options.text: Thicker walls with more muscle and elastic tissue

questions.explanation: Arteries have thicker, more muscular and elastic walls to cope with high pressure; veins are thinner and valved.

questions.stem: Which process pulls water through cell walls in the leaf, generating tension?
questions.explanation: Evaporation draws water from cell walls by capillary action, creating the tension that pulls water up the xylem.

questions.scenario: A student draws a dicot root section from a micrograph.
questions.stem: Which structures should be identified?

options.text: Vascular bundles, xylem, phloem, cortex and epidermis

questions.explanation: A dicot root section shows vascular tissue (xylem and phloem), cortex and epidermis.

questions.stem: Which feature best distinguishes an artery from a vein in a micrograph?

options.text: Wall thickness relative to lumen diameter

questions.explanation: Arteries and veins are distinguished by the thickness of the wall relative to the lumen diameter.

questions.stem: Occlusion of a coronary artery reduces blood flow to the:
questions.explanation: Coronary arteries supply the heart muscle, so their occlusion reduces the heart's blood supply.

questions.stem: Which combination lists adaptations of xylem vessels?

options.text: No cell contents, lignified walls, pits, open end walls

questions.explanation: Xylem vessels lack contents, have lignified walls, pits and incomplete/absent end walls for water transport.

questions.scenario: A vein is compressed by a contracting muscle.
questions.stem: The valves ensure that blood:

options.text: Flows backward away from the heart

questions.explanation: Valves prevent backflow, so muscle compression pushes venous blood toward the heart.

questions.stem: Capillaries allow exchange between the blood and:
questions.explanation: Capillaries are adapted for exchange of materials between blood and the internal or external environment.

questions.stem: When annotating a stem plan diagram, students should add:

options.text: The main functions of the labelled structures

questions.explanation: Students annotate the diagram with the main functions of the structures such as xylem and phloem.

questions.stem: Tissue fluid is formed at capillaries by:
questions.explanation: Tissue fluid forms by pressure filtration of plasma, promoted by high blood pressure at the arteriole end.

questions.scenario: At the venule end of a capillary, blood pressure is lower.
questions.stem: What happens to most tissue fluid here?

options.text: It drains back into the capillary

questions.explanation: Lower pressure at the venule end allows tissue fluid to drain back into the capillary.

questions.stem: Tissue fluid differs from blood plasma mainly in that tissue fluid has:

options.text: Fewer large plasma proteins and no blood cells

questions.explanation: Tissue fluid resembles plasma but lacks most large proteins and cells, which stay in the capillaries.

questions.scenario: Excess tissue fluid is not all reabsorbed by the capillaries.
questions.stem: Where does this excess drain?
questions.explanation: Excess tissue fluid drains into lymph ducts, which have valves and thin walls, and returns to the blood circulation.

questions.stem: Which correctly describes the circulation of a bony fish compared with a mammal?

options.text: Fish have single circulation; mammals have double circulation
Fish have double circulation; mammals have single

questions.explanation: Bony fish have single circulation (blood passes the heart once per circuit); mammals have double circulation.

questions.stem: Which structure sets the rhythm of the mammalian heartbeat?
questions.explanation: The pacemaker (sinoatrial node) initiates and sets the rhythm of the heartbeat.

questions.stem: The role of the atrioventricular valves is to:

options.text: Prevent backflow of blood from ventricles into atria
Separate the left and right sides
Supply the heart muscle with blood

questions.explanation: Atrioventricular valves prevent blood flowing back from the ventricles into the atria, ensuring unidirectional flow.

questions.stem: The cardiac cycle is initiated by the:
questions.explanation: The sinoatrial node (pacemaker) initiates the sequence of events of the cardiac cycle.

questions.scenario: A blood pressure reading is given as systolic over diastolic.
questions.stem: Systolic pressure is measured when the:
questions.explanation: Systolic pressure is the higher pressure during ventricular contraction; diastolic is during relaxation.

questions.scenario: In spring, before leaves open, a deciduous tree still moves water up its stem.
questions.stem: What mechanism generates this movement when transpiration is low?

options.text: Root pressure from active transport of mineral ions

questions.explanation: Root pressure, a positive pressure generated by active transport of mineral ions, moves water when transpiration is insufficient.

questions.stem: Root pressure is described as a positive pressure potential because it:

options.text: Pushes water upward, unlike the tension of transpiration
Is the same as transpiration pull

questions.explanation: Root pressure is a positive pressure that pushes water up, generated by active transport of ions into the xylem.

questions.stem: Which adaptation of phloem sieve tube elements eases the flow of sap?

options.text: Reduced cytoplasm, no nucleus and sieve plates
Complete end walls with no pores

questions.explanation: Sieve tube elements have reduced cytoplasm, no nucleus and sieve plates, easing the flow of sap.

questions.stem: Companion cells support phloem transport because they have:

options.text: Many mitochondria to power loading of sap

questions.explanation: Companion cells have many mitochondria and are linked by plasmodesmata, supporting the loading of carbon compounds into sieve tubes.

questions.scenario: In phloem, carbon compounds are loaded at sources and removed at sinks.
questions.stem: This overall movement of sap is called:
questions.explanation: Translocation is the movement of sap in the phloem from sources to sinks.

questions.stem: An advantage of double circulation in mammals is that:

options.text: Blood can be repressurized after the lungs, giving efficient delivery
Blood passes the heart only once per circuit

questions.explanation: Double circulation lets blood be repressurized by the heart after passing the lungs, giving efficient delivery to tissues.

questions.stem: The septum of the mammalian heart functions to:
questions.explanation: The septum separates the left and right sides, keeping oxygenated and deoxygenated blood apart.
